content: The reconstructed-band phenomena described above—Rashba-induced isolated extremely flat band pairs near K^{\pm}, the sixteen-Dirac-point structure in the single-valley four-band spectrum, and the computed Chern-number phase diagrams for isolated active bands—are valid in the continuum two-decoupled-valley description of twisted bilayer graphene that applies at sufficiently small twist angles and require being near the first magic angle so that (i) the eight active flat bands are well separated from remote bands by appreciable gaps and (ii) the active-band bandwidth is comparable to or smaller than typical proximate energy scales (Rashba \sim 16 meV, Ising \sim 1 meV, sublattice u \sim 1\text{–}2 meV); if the twist angle is too far from the magic angle so that the active-band bandwidth is much larger than these proximate couplings, then the Rashba coupling will not reconstruct the active bands and the reported band topology and extreme flatness need not occur.

Twisted trilayer graphene in a monolayer-on-AB-bilayer stacking geometry simultaneously exhibits (i) nearly flat moiré bands near a magic angle of about $1.12^\circ$, (ii) twist-induced gaps at Dirac points that permit valley-resolved Chern-number assignment for the central bands, and (iii) valley Chern numbers that can be tuned by varying the twist angle $\theta$ and a perpendicular-field-induced layer potential difference $V$; these properties make the system a promising platform to study strongly correlated physics in topologically nontrivial flat bands. The absence of the stacking symmetries present in more symmetric graphene moiré stacks implies that correlated states in the monolayer-on-bilayer geometry can differ from those in twisted bilayer graphene and twisted double-bilayer graphene.

The narrow low-energy bands in twisted gapped honeycomb bilayers such as hBN originate from resonant tunneling and confinement in local homopolar stacking regions where atoms of the same chemical species lie on top of each other; these regions host in-gap bound-like states that populate energies inside the nominal monolayer gap, and this confinement mechanism is distinct from the interference-based magic-angle mechanism of twisted bilayer graphene, as evidenced by the numerical observation that artificially setting interlayer hopping amplitudes that couple heteropolar dimers (i.e., hoppings between atoms of opposite chemical polarity, for example $w_{\mathrm{AB}'}$ and $w_{\mathrm{BA}'}$) to zero removes the hBN low-energy flat bands, whereas analogous simplifications in twisted graphene produce completely flat bands at the magic angle, demonstrating different confinement physics in gapped versus gapless bilayers.

Continuum-model calculations for mirror-symmetric A-tw-A magic-angle twisted trilayer graphene (MATTG), consisting of three graphene monolayers sequentially twisted by $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ with $\theta\approx1.57^\circ$, yield (a) mirror-symmetry-protected flat bands derived from mirror-symmetric combinations of outer-layer hopping and (b) coexisting gapless Dirac bands with opposite mirror character, such that hybridization between the flat and Dirac bands is forbidden when mirror symmetry is present. The flat bands can be mathematically reduced to magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene (MATBG)-like bands with an effective twist angle $\theta/\sqrt{2}$, implying a larger magic angle for MATTG of $\theta_{\mathrm{MATTG}}\approx1.6^\circ$. A perpendicular electric displacement field $D$ breaks the mirror symmetry and hybridizes the flat and Dirac bands, enabling in situ electrical control of the flat-band bandwidth and topology. These calculations neglect direct coupling between the topmost and bottommost graphene layers and use interlayer hopping parameters $w=0.1\ \mathrm{eV}$ and $w'=0.08\ \mathrm{eV}$, and they model the displacement field through an imposed interlayer potential difference $\Delta V=dD/\varepsilon_0$ with interlayer spacing $d\approx0.3\ \mathrm{nm}$ and vacuum permittivity $\varepsilon_0$.

Continuum-model electronic-structure calculations of magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene that include lattice relaxation driven by atomic-registry-dependent interlayer coupling reproduce the key experimental spectroscopic trends: the reconstructed geometry obtained from relaxation (enlarged AB/BA regions and reduced AA regions) yields broadened low-energy flat bands and increased energetic isolation of the flat-band manifold from remote bands relative to the unreconstructed rigid-geometry calculation. The implemented calculations omit explicit spatially non-uniform heterostrain and therefore do not reproduce additional fine splitting of the flat-band peaks (for example, three-subpeak structure) seen in some experimental spectra, which can be induced by heterostrain effects.

Modeling hBN-aligned twisted bilayer graphene near the magic angle $\theta_{\mathrm{M}}\simeq1.063^\circ$ by the Bistritzer–MacDonald moiré continuum model with interlayer tunneling parameters $u=0.077\ \mathrm{eV}$ and $u'=0.11\ \mathrm{eV}$ and adding a uniform sublattice-staggering on-site term of magnitude $\Delta/2$ to mimic hBN alignment reproduces the single-particle flat-band spectrum features relevant for computing the intrinsic capacitance; this continuum modeling neglects atomic relaxation, strain, lattice reconstruction, screening, and interaction-driven band renormalization, so quantitative predictions for the exact depth and location of capacitance extrema may differ in experimental devices. Bistritzer, R. & MacDonald, A. H., PNAS 108, 12233 (2011)

Atomistic lattice relaxation of TDBG computed by minimizing the REBO intra-layer and Kolmogorov–Crespi inter-layer classical potentials (with the FIRE algorithm termination threshold for forces per atom set to 10^−6 eV/atom) has a decisive effect on the low-energy electronic structure: including relaxation increases the gaps between the flat-band manifold and excited states by roughly an order of magnitude near the magic angle θ* = 1.30°, strongly suppresses the inter-sublattice AA moiré tunneling amplitude w_{AA} (reducing the ratio w_{AA}/w_{AB} where w_{AB} denotes the AB moiré coupling), and reduces the influence of the Bernal-bilayer trigonal-warping parameter γ_3, the particle–hole asymmetry parameter γ_4, and the intrinsic symmetric polarization on the flat-band dispersion in the vicinity of θ*. Quantitatively, including relaxation changes the band-structure metrics so that at θ* the half-bandwidth/bandgap ratio—where "half-bandwidth" denotes one-half of the flat-manifold bandwidth E_W and "bandgap" denotes the relevant gap to excited states such as Δ_e or Δ_h—is 14.7%, a value comparable to the corresponding metric reported for magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene.

The assembly principles consisting of sibling-layer creation by partial pick-up and controlled rotation, hBN flip-over for surface-termination control, and neighboring-graphite-guided principal-axis alignment are applicable beyond monolayer graphene/hBN heterostructures. The same deterministic alignment strategy was used to fabricate low-angle twisted bilayer graphene and ABC-stacked trilayer graphene devices, demonstrating that the workflow generalizes to other twist-sensitive moiré platforms for flat-band engineering and correlated-electron studies.

For twisted bilayer graphene at the first magic twist angle $\theta=1.08^\circ$, the single-particle electronic structure computed by density functional theory (DFT) shows that the narrow flat-band manifold near the Fermi energy and the energy gaps at the $\Gamma$ point separating that manifold from higher- and lower-energy bands are reproduced only when full atomic relaxation is included, where full relaxation includes both in-plane ($x$-$y$) and out-of-plane ($z$) atomic displacements relative to the initial twisted but flat bilayer geometry. For the fully relaxed geometry at $\theta=1.08^\circ$, the reported flat-band bandwidth is $\sim 20\,\text{meV}$, the gap separating the flat-band manifold from the higher-energy bands is $\approx 26\,\text{meV}$, and the gap separating the flat-band manifold from the lower-energy bands is $\approx 16\,\text{meV}$, and tight-binding calculations evaluated on the same relaxed atomic coordinates yield consistent low-energy bands. For the unrelaxed flat bilayer geometry these $\Gamma$-point gaps are absent or strongly underestimated, and for constrained relaxations at $\theta=1.08^\circ$ the in-plane-only relaxed geometry (fixed $z$ coordinates) yields zero gaps while the out-of-plane-only relaxed geometry (fixed $x$-$y$ coordinates) yields underestimated gaps of order $\sim 2\,\text{meV}$ (upper side) and $\sim 14\,\text{meV}$ (lower side).

The single‑valley continuum Hamiltonian for magic‑angle twisted bilayer graphene with the top graphene layer nearly aligned to an h‑BN substrate is $H=H_{TBG}+H_{hBN}$ with $$ H_{hBN}=\sum_{\mathbf{k}} M\,\psi_t^{\dagger}(\mathbf{k})\mu_z\psi_t(\mathbf{k}) +\sum_{j=1}^{6}\psi_t^{\dagger}(\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{Q}_j')V_j\psi_t(\mathbf{k}), $$ where $\psi_{t}(\mathbf{k})$ and $\psi_{b}(\mathbf{k})$ are two‑component spinors describing the $A,B$ sublattice degrees of freedom on the top and bottom graphene layers, $\mu_z$ is the Pauli matrix acting in the $A,B$ sublattice space, $M$ is the staggered sublattice mass induced on the top graphene layer by near alignment to h‑BN, $V_j$ are the weaker moiré potential matrices generated by the lattice mismatch between h‑BN and graphene with typical matrix norms $|V_j|\lesssim 10\,$meV, and the dominant TBG moiré interlayer coupling scale is $w_1\approx110\,$meV. For parameter estimates $M\sim15$–$17\,$meV and $|V_j|\ll w_1$, explicit numerical diagonalization of $H$ in the moiré Brillouin zone (MBZ) produces gapped Dirac crossings at the MBZ corners in each graphene valley and yields an isolated pair of active bands (one conduction, one valence) per valley; for valley labeled $+$ the computed Chern numbers are $C=+1$ for the conduction active band and $C=-1$ for the valence active band (the opposite signs occur in the time‑reversed valley). For the representative parameters $M=15\,$meV and twist angle $\theta_M=1.20^\circ$ the computed indirect gap between these conduction and valence active bands is of order $\sim5\,$meV.

In atomistic tight-binding calculations performed on relaxed twisted double bilayer graphene (TDBG) geometries, the low-energy moiré flat bands of relaxed TDBG are generally narrower than the corresponding low-energy flat bands of twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) for twist angles $\\theta>1.1^\\circ$, and the computed flat-band bandwidth for relaxed TDBG attains a local minimum near $\\theta\\approx1.25^\\circ$; this local minimum in bandwidth is identified as the first (primary) magic angle of TDBG in these calculations, and at $\\theta\\approx1.25^\\circ$ the TDBG low-energy flat-band bandwidth can be more than a factor of two smaller than the TBG flat-band bandwidth at the same twist angle.

Proposition — Transfer of disorder threshold: the disorder amplitude scale of order $40\,$meV reported in the twisted-bilayer-graphene literature as the approximate strength at which magic-angle flat-band topology is destroyed is adopted as an approximate upper bound for the disorder amplitude at which ensemble-averaged total Chern number of flat bands in twisted rhombohedral graphite is expected to vanish; claim: by assuming comparability of the remote band gaps protecting the flat bands, the spatial structure and valley separation of the wavefunctions, and the sensitivity to intervalley scattering between twisted bilayer graphene and the rhombohedral-stacking-fault system, the same order-of-magnitude threshold ($\sim40\,$meV) is used as the disorder scale beyond which topological protection breaks down in the rhombohedral stacking-fault system studied here. [46]

The numerically identified twist angle at which the two primary moiré bands become nearly flat in twisted few-layer graphite is about $1.05^\circ$, the same numerical value as the principal magic angle obtained for twisted bilayer graphene within the same continuum description.

For the twisted monolayer–bilayer geometry defined as a graphene monolayer rotated by angle $\\theta$ and placed on top of an AB-stacked graphene bilayer (denoted SM-(AB)$_2$), the moiré system at its magic-angle moiré geometry (near $\\theta\\simeq1.1^{\\circ}$) exhibits isolated low-energy flat bands, but these flat bands are quantitatively less flat than those of relaxed twisted bilayer graphene: the SM-(AB)$_2$ flat-band manifold has larger bandwidth and a smaller zero-energy DOS peak than magic-angle TBLG. Layer-resolved density of states $\\rho_{\\ell}(E)$ shows that the flat-band spectral weight is strongly localized on the twisted monolayer (top layer) while it is comparatively broadened and delocalized across the AB bilayer (the two bottom layers), producing a spatially separated localized–delocalized electronic character across layers.

For commensurate twisted bilayer graphene supercells computed at 33 unique commensurate twist angles spanning 0.88° ≤ θ ≤ 21.79°, the ab initio Wannier-derived tight-binding calculations and automated flat-band detection identify a maximized flat-band likelihood p(θ) at a magic angle θ* ≈ 1.1°, with the emergence of low-dispersion (near-flat) bands near the Fermi level in the single-particle TB band structures at that angle.

For magic‑angle twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) in which the top graphene layer is nearly aligned to a hexagonal boron nitride (h‑BN) substrate, taking continuum‑model parameters twist angle $ \theta_{M}\approx 1.20^{\circ} $ and an h‑BN induced staggered sublattice mass $ M\approx 15\ \mathrm{meV} $, the in‑plane two‑fold rotation symmetry $C_{2}$ is explicitly broken by the h‑BN alignment; this $C_{2}$ breaking gaps the Dirac band crossings that occur at the corners of the moiré Mini Brillouin Zone (MBZ) within each graphene valley, producing for a single valley an isolated conduction moiré band and an isolated valence moiré band that carry integer Chern numbers of opposite sign (conduction band Chern number $C=+1$, valence band Chern number $C=-1$). For the numerical continuum‑model diagonalization with the above parameter choice the single‑valley direct band gap between the conduction and valence bands is of order a few meV (numerical example $\sim 5\ \mathrm{meV}$ when $M=15\ \mathrm{meV}$), so the two active bands in one valley are well separated from each other and from other bands and have well‑defined Berry curvature and integer Chern indices.

The superconducting behavior reported here is realized in a vertically stacked hBN/BLG/hBN Moiré superlattice in which the two graphene layers forming the bilayer graphene are not intentionally twisted (the BLG is non‑twisted) and the alignment angle between one hBN sheet and the BLG is near zero; this single‑device demonstration shows that fine tuning of the inter‑graphene twist to a so‑called magic angle is not required in this device architecture to obtain superconductivity by electrostatic (back‑gate) doping.

For continuum-model electronic-structure calculations of magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene that explicitly incorporate lattice relaxation via atomic-registry-dependent interlayer coupling leading to enlarged AB/BA regions and shrunken AA regions, the computed low-energy flat-band manifold exhibits qualitatively increased bandwidth (broader flat bands) and increased energetic separation from remote bands in the reconstructed geometry relative to the unreconstructed rigid geometry, and this qualitative trend is robust to moderate variations of model parameters even when spatially non-uniform heterostrain is not included in the calculation; here "continuum model" refers to the Bistritzer–MacDonald-type moiré Hamiltonian augmented by a registry-dependent interlayer tunneling parameterization and a relaxation-derived displacement field. [18-21]

A dense‑angle atomistic numerical survey of fully relaxed twisted bilayer graphene structures with twist angles sampled down to approximately 0.22^\circ, using the relaxation procedure and the p_z tight‑binding Green’s‑function electronic‑structure methods described above, is sufficient to detect the reappearance of isolated flat bands (additional magic‑angle phenomena) if such isolated flat bands would occur in realistic relaxed samples; therefore the absence of observed isolated flat bands on the computed angle grid implies their practical non‑existence in reconstructed experimental samples within the sampled angle range.

The use of the empirical observation that hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) substrates can induce single-particle energy gaps as large as ~30 meV in monolayer graphene to argue that alignment to hBN can substantially modify the energetic competition among correlated many-body ground states in flat-band twisted bilayer graphene (tBLG) assumes that the single-particle sublattice-symmetry-breaking potential effects of hBN alignment on monolayer graphene are at least qualitatively transferable to the correlated, narrow-bandwidth, multi-band moiré electronic structure of tBLG; this extrapolation presumes that comparable gap scales and their influence can persist in the moiré flat-band environment. Symbols: "hBN" denotes hexagonal boron nitride, "meV" denotes milli–electron-volt, and tBLG denotes twisted bilayer graphene.

Numerical calculations for alternating-twist four-layer and five-layer graphene, using the same drive parameters $A=0.08\,a^{-1}$ and $\hbar\Omega=6\,\mathrm{eV}$ together with lattice-relaxation-informed effective interlayer tunneling ratios and magic-angle conditions, show that the central Floquet flat band remains gapped and topological at the first magic angle in both layer numbers. In the four-layer case the decomposition contains a twisted-bilayer-graphene subsystem and a nonmagic twisted-bilayer-graphene-like subsystem, and in the five-layer case it contains a twisted-bilayer-graphene subsystem together with an additional non-twisted-bilayer subsystem and a graphene subsystem. The corresponding phase diagrams contain additional gap closings at $\Gamma_{M}$ and $K_M$ arising from extra Dirac cones, but the lattice-relaxed first-magic-angle parameter points lie inside gapped topological regions. Floquet driving therefore produces topological flat bands in four- and five-layer alternating-twist multilayer graphene under realistic relaxation-modified parameters.

Let the "magic angle" denote a twist angle near $\sim1^{\circ}$ at which twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) exhibits very flat bands in realistic models that include lattice relaxation. The proposition is that a tight-binding band-structure computation for TBG at twist angle $\theta=1.5^{\circ}$, obtained without including lattice relaxation effects in the model, yields a band dispersion sufficiently similar in the low-energy flat-band window to the relaxed magic-angle dispersion that transport and UMR trends computed at $\theta=1.5^{\circ}$ can serve as a qualitative proxy for the magic-angle regime when discussing enhancement of UMR by flat bands. [60]

The ab initio observation of band inversions that entangle bands originating both above and below the single-layer Dirac point implies that minimal phenomenological or few-band effective models intended to capture the symmetry and fragile topology of twisted bilayer graphene must include complementary bands from both sides of the Dirac point (i.e., include neighboring dispersive bands above and below the flat-band manifold); omitting these neighboring dispersive bands on either side of the flat bands will fail to reproduce the symmetry-entangling inversions and therefore cannot correctly capture the fragile-topological features observed in the ab initio spectrum.

Consider a magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene device that is placed on a hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) substrate with a relative crystallographic alignment angle between the graphene moiré lattice and the hBN lattice denoted by \theta_{\mathrm{rel}}; if \theta_{\mathrm{rel}}>10^{\circ} (ten degrees), then any single-particle sublattice-staggered potential arising from alignment between the moiré superlattice and the hBN lattice is negligible for low-energy electronic states, and consequently the substrate does not impose a significant explicit single-particle twofold rotation (C_{2}) symmetry breaking term in the effective low-energy Hamiltonian of the sample.

Comparing electronic band structures computed for the relaxed atomic geometry and for the unrelaxed rigidly twisted lattice at the magic angle, lattice relaxation increases the gaps between remote bands and the flat bands, increases the flat-band bandwidth, and enhances intervalley scattering (scattering between states derived from the moiré $K$ and $K'$ valleys), with the relaxation-induced intervalley coupling producing a splitting of the two Dirac points of approximately $0.19\ \mathrm{meV}$ in the computed single-particle band structure.

High-resolution STM topographic images taken at the probe locations in the studied MATBG devices show no detectable crystallographic alignment between the graphene moiré pattern and the underlying hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) substrate; the proposition is that this absence of locally observed hBN alignment is sufficient evidence to conclude that there is no hBN-induced global breaking of the two-fold rotation ($C_2$) symmetry—i.e., no effective sublattice-staggered potential from hBN alignment—acting anywhere in the device region that gives rise to the observed correlated Chern insulating phases, under the assumptions that (i) any hBN alignment strong enough to produce a relevant $C_2$-breaking staggered potential would be detectable by the performed STM topography at the probed spatial locations and (ii) such alignment is not present in unprobed regions that nevertheless influence the local measured spectroscopic gaps.
